mnemonics: P-NP: 'Problems Nobody's Patented' (for Nobody can solve NP quickly, but verifying is Possible)
Cook-Levin: 'Chef Levin Cooks up SAT' (to remember SAT was the first NP-Complete problem proven by Cook/Levin)
Reduction Route: 'A to B is easy if B is at least as hard as A' (for if A ≤p B and B is easy, then A is easy)
keyPoints: P is the set of problems solvable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
NP is the set of problems verifiable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
A problem is NP-Complete if it is in NP and every problem in NP is polynomial-time reducible to it.
commonMistakes: Believing NP stands for 'Non-Polynomial'. It stands for Nondeterministic Polynomial time.
Assuming that if P=NP, all problems would become trivial to solve. While it would be revolutionary, the polynomials might still be too high for practical use.
Thinking a reduction from A to B proves A is harder than B. Actually, if A ≤p B, it means B is at least as hard as A.
